content:
4 min read
Just now

--

A practical way to find out exactly what breaks before you move a WordPress site to a new PHP or WordPress version.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

There’s a particular kind of dread every WordPress developer learns to recognize.
It arrives as an email from the host: “PHP 7.4 has reached end of life. Your sites will be moved to PHP 8.x on the 30th.” Or it arrives as your own good intentions — you want the speed and security of PHP 8.4, so you stage the upgrade, take a breath, and click. And then: a white screen. Somewhere across the forty plugins and three themes on the site, a single line of code calls a function PHP removed years ago, and now the entire site is down.
Here’s the part that makes it so maddening: the broken code is almost never yours. It’s buried inside third-party plugins and themes you didn’t write, can’t easily read, and definitely didn’t memorize the version-compatibility history of. You inherited the risk the moment you installed them.
So the real question isn’t “will this upgrade break something?” It’s “what, specifically, will break — and can I see it before a real user does?”
This is a guide to answering that, using an open-source tool I built called Pressready.
Why “we tested it on staging” gives false confidence
Staging is necessary but not sufficient. It tells you that something broke — eventually, and only for the code paths you happened to click through. A fatal error hiding in an admin screen, a checkout edge case, or a cron job won’t reveal itself during a five-minute manual smoke test. It waits for production traffic.
To catch breakage reliably you have to read all the code, not just the parts you exercise. That’s static analysis — and it’s exactly the kind of unglamorous, exhaustive work software should do for you.
Upgrades break along two axes
A complete readiness check has to cover both:

• The PHP axis. Language features get removed between major versions — create_function() and each() are gone in PHP 8.0, and plenty more shifted in 8.1–8.4 — or they change behaviour in subtle, results-altering ways.

• The WordPress axis. Each WordPress release deprecates and eventually removes core APIs that plugins quietly depend on.
Pressready checks both in a single pass: it runs PHPCompatibility for the PHP side and a custom analyzer — driven by a generated dataset of WordPress core deprecations — for the WordPress side.
Seeing it work
You don’t need to add anything to the project. Grab the standalone build, or run it through Docker:
# Single self-contained file, just needs PHP
curl -L https://github.com/itzmekhokan/pressready/releases/latest/download/pressready.phar -o pressready.phar
chmod +x pressready.phar
./pressready.phar --php=8.4 --wp=6.9 --path=wp-content# Or nothing-to-install Docker
docker run --rm -v "$PWD":/src -w /src ghcr.io/itzmekhokan/pressready:1 --php=8.4 --path=wp-content(There’s also Composer, Homebrew, a WP-CLI command, and a GitHub Action — same engine everywhere.)
The output is grouped by component, with a file:line and a severity for every finding, so you can triage instead of drowning:

• Fatal — a symbol the target version removed. This is the white-screen tier. Fix before you upgrade.

• Risky — code that still runs but may now produce wrong results.

• Deprecated — features still shimmed for now; clean them up on your own schedule.
The question you actually have
Most upgrade decisions aren’t binary. They’re “how far can I move right now, and what’s the first thing that stops me?” Pressready’s matrix mode answers precisely that, grading every version in a range from one scan:
pressready --php=7.4-8.4 --matrix --path=wp-contentPHP
7.4 ✓ clear
8.0 ✗ 1 fatal
...
Safe through PHP 7.4 · first break: PHP 8.0Now the conversation with your client or your team changes from “we think it’s probably fine” to “we can safely go to 7.4 today; one plugin blocks 8.0, and here’s its name and line number.”
Making it stick
Two features turn this from a one-time audit into a habit:

• Baseline mode. Inheriting a site with hundreds of pre-existing warnings? Snapshot them and only fail on new problems going forward — so you can adopt the tool without a cleanup marathon.

• CI integration. A GitHub Actions gate is two lines; it also emits SARIF for code scanning and a GitLab Code Quality report. Every pull request gets checked, so nobody reintroduces a fatal.
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
- uses: itzmekhokan/pressready@v1
with:
php: "8.4"
fail-on: fatalWhere it stops
In the interest of honesty: this is static analysis. It finds symbol-level removals and deprecations across the whole stack — not runtime logic regressions or data-shape problems. PHP 8.2–8.4 detection rides on a pre-release engine you opt into at install. And the WordPress dataset is only as current as its last regeneration (it warns you if you target a newer release than it knows). For the specific question “is this site safe to move to PHP 8.4,” though, it covers the scary 90% that actually takes sites down.
The takeaway
PHP and WordPress upgrades don’t have to be a gamble. Reading the whole stack first turns “let’s hope” into a concrete punch list: here are the fatals, here’s the plugin causing each one, and here’s exactly how far you can safely go today.
Pressready is free and open source. If you run it against a real site, I’d genuinely like to know what it caught — and what you wish it caught that it doesn’t yet.

• GitHub: github.com/itzmekhokan/pressready

• Docs and examples: itzmekhokan.github.io/pressready
